output_text:
Revised the section to eliminate heavy repetition, tighten phrasing, and flag one substantive issue with the cross-encoder comparison.

**Main issues**

1. **Severe repetition / anaphora bloat.** Phrases like "our approach for ranking clarification questions," "the neural ranker," "candidate clarification questions," "For evaluation," and "achieves an MRR / precision at one of" are repeated to the point of obscuring content. Roughly 40% of the section is filler.
2. **Bi-encoder beating a cross-encoder is unusual and under-discussed.** Cross-encoders almost always outperform bi-encoders on pairwise ranking when trained on the same data, because they attend jointly over the two inputs. The section reports the opposite (0.612 vs. 0.594 MRR; 0.487 vs. 0.471 P@1) without explanation. Either (a) the cross-encoder was under-tuned / trained on different data, (b) the gap is within noise, or (c) something is wrong. You need to state training parity, report variance/significance, and justify the result. As written, a reviewer will not believe the claim.
3. **"Slightly outperforms" is not supported.** A 1.8-point MRR / 1.6-point P@1 gap on 2,400 examples needs a significance test (paired bootstrap or randomization) before you claim an improvement. Otherwise soften to "is comparable to."
4. **Missing methodological details.** No mention of: encoder backbone (BERT? a multilingual model?), negative sampling strategy, margin value, number of candidates per source, how the 2,400-sentence test set was constructed, inter-annotator agreement on the usefulness judgments, or which language pairs are involved (the setup mentions English source but no target).
5. **"Learning to rank … pairwise preferences … highest ranked most likely to resolve the ambiguity"** triples the same idea. Pairwise preferences is a *training* formulation; "most likely to resolve ambiguity" is the *objective*. Separate them.
6. **Motivation paragraph asserts but does not cite.** "Source-side ambiguity often leads to translation errors" needs a citation.
7. **Inference cost** is the usual reason to prefer a bi-encoder over a cross-encoder. If that is your real argument, say so — it reframes a near-tie as a win.

**Revised text**

> We describe our approach to ranking clarification questions for interactive machine translation. Source-side ambiguity is a known cause of translation errors [cite], and our system surfaces the most informative question to the source-language author before translation is committed. We cast ranking as a learning-to-rank problem trained with pairwise preferences over candidate clarification questions.
>
> The ranker is a bi-encoder: source sentence and candidate question are encoded independently with [backbone], and the score is the dot product of the two encodings. We train with a margin loss; positives are questions that human annotators judged useful for translation, negatives are questions they judged not useful. [Add: margin, negative sampling, batch size, annotation protocol, inter-annotator agreement.]
>
> We evaluate on a held-out set of 2,400 source sentences, reporting mean reciprocal rank (MRR) and precision at one (P@1). We compare against BM25 and a cross-encoder trained on the same data.
>
> | Model          | MRR    | P@1   |
> |----------------|--------|-------|
> | BM25           | 0.418  | 0.301 |
> | Cross-encoder  | 0.594  | 0.471 |
> | Bi-encoder (ours) | 0.612 | 0.487 |
>
> Our bi-encoder substantially outperforms BM25 and is comparable to the cross-encoder while admitting precomputed candidate encodings, giving a [N]× inference speedup at deployment. [If a paired significance test supports it, replace "is comparable to" with "modestly outperforms the cross-encoder (p < 0.05, paired bootstrap)."]

**Uncertainty disclosure**

- I cannot verify whether the 0.612 vs. 0.594 gap is statistically significant; that depends on per-example scores I don't have.
- The claim that cross-encoders usually beat bi-encoders is the standard finding in IR/retrieval literature (e.g., Reimers & Gurevych 2019; Humeau et al. 2020) but I have not re-checked those citations here.
- The inference-speed framing is a recommendation, not a fact about your system — only adopt it if it matches your actual deployment story.
